thread.title: Cash for scrappage of petrol and diesel cars is latest Fine Gael election pledge
thread.title_full: Cash for scrappage of petrol and diesel cars is latest Fine Gael election pledge

author: Senan Molony
title: Cash for scrappage of petrol and diesel cars is latest Fine Gael election pledge
text:
Cash for scrappage of petrol and diesel cars is latest Fine Gael election pledge
Party also plans for more charge points and cheaper EVs as part of an environmentally friendly government
Motorists who convert to electric vehicles will receive cash benefits for the scrappage of petrol and diesel cars, under a new election promise being unveiled by Fine Gael.
Fine Gael says it is committed to examining the establishment of a vehicle scrappage scheme to allow drivers trade in their cars for a greener model.
The party wants to assert that it can be trusted on climate change and the environment – without the need to involve the Greens.
Simon Harris’s party will commit to the scrappage scheme to replace petrol and diesel cars with electric vehicles, with cash benefits for those who convert.
A government-supported scrappage scheme was previously introduced by former Labour Party finance minister Ruairi Quinn – but it was before the advent of mainstream electric cars.
Dublin Fingal East candidate Alan Farrell TD said: “We have had scrappage schemes before and they have worked in changing behaviours.
“We want more people to use electric vehicles and we know people want to use them more. But they are expensive for a lot of people.”
If Fine Gael is put back into government by the people, “we will look at the introduction of a scrappage scheme to replace older, polluting vehicles with electric cars”, Mr Farrell added.
It is also understood that old motorbikes and scooters would be scrapped to be replaced by non-polluting electric bikes.
And Fine Gael says it would completely overhaul the public transport fleet, starting with buses, aimed at promoting a cleaner transport system.
The party is also promising to increase the number of publicly available EV charging points fourfold to nearly 10,000, ensuring better coverage nationwide.
It also wants to review customs duties on second-hand EV imports to make electric vehicles more affordable.
Under the Climate Action Plan, the Government initially set a target of having 175,000 electric passenger cars on the road by the end of next year, which would rise to 845,000 by the end of 2030.
“We are making good progress on meeting our 2025 target but we have to make sure we can help make through the climate transition,” Mr Farrell added.
“The world is on fire and we have to ensure we take the necessary steps to help people with the change. We are doing that through the retrofitting grants and helping people make their homes warmer.
“We are also doing it through reduced public transport fees to encourage more people taking the bus or train to work.”
He said Fine Gael is also committing to a Bike-to-College scheme to help students with the cost of a new bike, matching the existing Bike-to-Work scheme for employees.
“We believe these practical measures can help move the dial,’’ he added.
The pledges will likely be viewed suspiciously by the Green Party, which has detected a definite intention on the part of Fine Gael and Fianna Fáil to replace it with another political partner in the next coalition.
